channel.title: Urban ecology on xpsong
channel.description: Recent content in Urban ecology on xpsong

item.title: Particulate matter mitigation via plants: Understanding complex relationships with leaf traits
An ecosystem service design loop for using vegetation to mitigate the urban heat island effect
Assessment of light adequacy for vertical farming in a tropical city
Benefits of trees in tropical cities
The natural capital of city trees
item.description:
Trees are one of the largest components of urban greenery, and contribute significantly to canopy cover in cities’ urban greening efforts. They are also associated with numerous benefits through the ‘ecosystem services’ they provide to city dwellers. For example, trees can lower temperatures by providing shade, mitigate air pollution by adsorbing pollutants, and reduce the risk of flooding by intercepting rainfall. Trees are thus a form of ‘natural capital’ that produce value for people.

Many cities have started to assess the status and health of these natural capital assets, which is an important step to incorporate the environment into holistic, national-scale planning. Many downstream benefits rely heavily on a tree’s physical structure, which is not constant but changes over its lifespan and between species. However, urban tree inventories in the tropics are few and poorly represented in allometric studies, despite a large diversity of plants in this climate region. While there is a large body of knowledge of tree structure and growth in natural forests, unique conditions in urban areas can result in allometric relationships that vary greatly from those in natural environments.

Finally, it is also crucial to weigh the benefits of trees against potential hazards and costs of management, and to assess how these effects change across time. There are also opportunities to examine how other plant traits relate to a tree’s physical structure, to provide generalisable findings that overcome the limitations of sampling many under-represented plant species. A better understanding of these relationships can help us maximise the cost-effectiveness of trees in the long run.

item.title: Ecosystem services in urban landscapes
item.description:
Urban greenery can help mitigate the negative impacts of urbanisation, and improve city liveability for urban dwellers. The benefits associated with greenery are realised through the provision of ‘ecosystem services’ such as evaporative cooling, rainfall interception, and green spaces for recreation.

Most research on urban ecosystem services has been conducted in temperate Europe and North America, but we know relatively little about how tropical cities can be designed to enhance ecosystem service provision. Tropical areas, particularly Southeast Asia, are rapidly becoming more urban, with cities growing larger and people living in higher densities. It is therefore important to understand how urban greenery can contribute to enhancing the sustainability and resilience of such cities.

This project uses new types of data and modelling approaches to understand how we can improve the ecological performance of urban greenery. Physical, ecological and socio-cultural parameters are used as inputs to model various types of ecosystem services. Landscape design is performed using 3D point cloud data, to achieve highly accurate representations of existing landscapes. The iterative process of model simulation and design feedback provides opportunities to improve designs based on scientific results.

item.title: Feasibility of urban farming in a compact city
item.description:
Food security is emerging as a serious threat in many parts of the world. In cities, common strategies to improve the resilience of food supplies are to diversify the sources of food, and to optimise local production. In compact cities where land is scarce, vertical and rooftop farming have been explored as an alternative to conventional agriculture. However, the success of such measures rely on their environmental, social and economic feasilibility within the local context.

My Honors Thesis assessed the suitability of light conditions along the vertical surfaces of buildings in Singapore, a dense and compact city in the tropics. These conditions were compared against the light requirements of several vegetable crops that require high amounts of light, and that form a significant proportion of the staple diet in many Asian countries. Plant physiological variables such as dark respiration (Rd), light compensation point, light saturation point, photosynthetic capacity (Amax) and quantum yield (Φ) were derived. These were used to calculate plant light requirements, or the ‘Daily Light Integral’, which ranged from 10–30 mol m-2 day-1. Depending on the building height, orientation and configuration, light conditions along vertical surfaces ranged from 2–35 mol m-2 day-1.
